chapter: 13.5 AI Safety: Scalable Oversight, Dangerous-Capability Evals & Frontier Safety
core_intuitions: The oversight gap: model task accuracy p_M outruns human verification accuracy p_H, so naive RLHF starts rewarding answers that LOOK right to a rater who cannot tell; scalable oversight's job is to lift the human curve (p_H^eff) with AI assistance, all resting on the verification-generation gap (checking is easier than producing).

The three scalable-oversight mechanisms are structurally DIFFERENT ways to exploit the verification-generation gap: debate = two adversaries decompose a hard judgment into local rebuttals a weak judge can follow; recursive reward modeling = recursively decompose a task so every human judgment stays within human competence; weak-to-strong generalization = weak labels ELICIT a strong model's latent knowledge instead of capping it.

Dangerous-capability evaluation measures marginal UPLIFT (with-model arm vs no-model control arm), not absolute capability, under STRONG elicitation, and is always a lower bound on true capability - a failed eval never proves safety.

Sandbagging and unfaithful CoT mean a model can actively look safer than it is: the most dangerous models can appear the safest, and optimizing against a reasoning monitor teaches the model to HIDE rather than to not-do.

Control vs. alignment: instead of making the model trustworthy, assume it may be adversarial and engineer scaffolding (trusted weak monitor + scarce human-audit budget + restricted actions) so a scheming model still cannot succeed - traded off against a usefulness cost.

Frontier-safety frameworks turn 'safe to deploy' into a hard gate: capability thresholds as tripwires, with a conservative safety BUFFER set below the real danger line (subtracting elicitation gap + between-checkpoint capability jumps) so safeguards trigger before a checkpoint quietly crosses the true threshold.

existing.name: oversight-gap-curves
existing.improvement: Renders cleanly and correctly in both light and dark themes. Two rising curves (p_M model accuracy, p_H unaided human) with the shaded oversight gap between them, the dashed p_H^eff AI-assisted-human curve lifting into the gap, and the goal caption. Accurately and legibly conveys the chapter's foundational intuition (model outruns verifier; scalable oversight lifts the human curve). No clipping, good reading order, theme-safe. Keep as-is.

existing.name: safety-case-argument-tree
existing.issue: Body text overflows the right edge of each of the three cards - clearly clipped in the dark-theme render (e.g. 'The model MIGHT be abl[e]', 'Dangerous-capability eval[s]', 'Scalable-oversight trainin[g]', 'no scheming on deceptio[n]'). The card body fills are also hardcoded white/near-white rather than theme-aware, so in dark theme they read as bright panels floating on black and the overflow onto the black background is stark.
existing.improvement: Reflow so every line of body text wraps INSIDE the card width with comfortable right padding (widen cards slightly and/or reduce body font and re-wrap the <tspan> lines so nothing crosses the card's right edge in either theme). Make the card body fill theme-aware (a subtle surface token in light, a raised-surface token in dark) instead of hardcoded white, keeping the three colored header bands (inability/control/trustworthiness) as the only saturated accents. Verify the 'increasing trust required' arrow and its caption do not collide with the bottom of the cards. Keep the structure otherwise - the claim-at-top, three-supporting-arguments, left-to-right increasing-trust ordering is correct and pedagogically right.

new_figures.name: three-oversight-mechanisms
new_figures.concept: Side-by-side structural comparison of debate, recursive reward modeling, and weak-to-strong generalization as three distinct ways to exploit the verification-generation gap.
new_figures.rationale: The section 'Three Scalable-Oversight Mechanisms' is ~40% of the chapter and its whole point is that debate, RRM, and W2SG are structurally DIFFERENT solutions to the same oversight-gap problem. Prose describes each in isolation; nothing lets a reader see them side by side and grasp that (a) debate decomposes via adversaries, (b) RRM decomposes via recursion, (c) W2SG elicits latent knowledge - three shapes, one goal. This is the single highest-leverage missing figure; it turns three paragraphs into one mental picture.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Three Scalable-Oversight Mechanisms', immediately under the section heading and before the 'Debate' subheading
new_figures.brief: A three-panel horizontal infographic, one panel per mechanism, sharing a common caption strip along the bottom that reads: 'All three recover a trustworthy signal ABOVE unaided human capability by exploiting the verification-generation gap (checking is easier than producing).' Give each panel a titled header band. Panel 1 - DEBATE: two adversary nodes labeled 'Debater A (defends answer 1)' and 'Debater B (defends answer 2)' facing each other with a two-way arrow of alternating turns (small numbered turn chips 1,2,3 between them), both feeding down into a single 'Judge (weak - cannot solve alone)' node; annotate 'each side surfaces the OTHER's flaw; judge only follows the chain of rebuttals'. Panel 2 - RECURSIVE REWARD MODELING: a decomposition tree - one 'Hard task (too hard to judge directly)' root splitting into 2-3 'subtask' children, one of which splits again into 'sub-subtasks'; put a small human-check icon/label at the leaf level and at each composition node ('human judges each piece + whether the composition is faithful - every judgment stays within human competence'); an upward 'aligned agent assists evaluation' arrow shows the amplification. Panel 3 - WEAK-TO-STRONG GENERALIZATION: a small 'Weak supervisor' box emitting 'weak (noisy) labels' arrow into a large 'Strong model' box whose interior shows a faint 'latent knowledge from pretraining' region; a bold arrow labeled 'fine-tune ELICITS latent knowledge' and an output marker sitting ABOVE the weak label level, annotated 'strong model outperforms its own supervisor (partial recovery = PGR)'. Keep the three panels visually parallel in size. Use ONLY theme tokens: neutral surfaces, one accent hue per panel used sparingly on the header band and key arrow. No invented numbers; the only integers are small turn/subtask counts (1,2,3). Everything must fit the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order within each panel and left-to-right panel order. Theme-safe fills for both light and dark.

new_figures.name: trusted-monitoring-control-loop
new_figures.concept: The AI-control deployment protocol: an untrusted strong model's action is scored by a trusted weak monitor and routed to accept / defer-to-trusted / human-audit under a scarce audit budget, with the safety-vs-usefulness trade-off it produces.
new_figures.rationale: The control-vs-alignment distinction is one of the chapter's most important and least intuitive ideas ('safety without trust'). The mechanism is inherently a routing FLOW - suspicion score gates three branches under a finite audit budget - which prose and code convey only linearly. A single flow diagram makes the 'weak model is the GUARD, not the student' inversion click and shows why cheap monitoring + a tiny audit budget catches most subversion. This is the load-bearing figure for the near-term 'control' arm of the later safety case.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Control vs. Alignment: Safety Without Trust', after the bulleted 'key building blocks of a control protocol' list and before the code block
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right flow diagram of one control step, with a small trade-off inset. Left: 'Untrusted model (strong, maybe scheming)' emits a 'proposed action (e.g. code submission)'. That arrow enters a 'Trusted monitor (weak, safe)' node which emits a 'suspicion score in [0,1]' onto a vertical suspicion gauge/threshold marker labeled 'audit threshold'. From the gauge, three routed branches: (1) LOW suspicion -> 'accept untrusted action' (fast path); (2) HIGH suspicion AND audit budget remaining -> 'human audit' node (draw a small depleting 'audit budget e.g. top ~2% of actions' meter beside it) whose outcome is 'safe' or 'BACKDOOR caught'; (3) HIGH suspicion BUT budget spent -> 'defer to trusted model output (weaker but safe) / resample'. Annotate the central inversion prominently: 'the WEAK model is the GUARD here, not the student (inverse of weak-to-strong)'. Add a compact inset in a corner: a small safety-vs-usefulness axes sketch (x = usefulness, y = P(catch backdoor)) with a single frontier curve and a dot, captioned 'more auditing/deferring -> safer but less useful'. Use theme tokens only; reserve one accent hue for the 'human audit / caught' path and a neutral tone for the fast-accept path. No invented benchmark numbers - the only literal is an illustrative '~2%' audit fraction and the [0,1] score range. Ensure the three branches do not overlap and the reading order (untrusted -> monitor -> three branches) is unambiguous. Legible in both themes.

new_figures.name: safety-buffer-tripwire
new_figures.concept: Why an eval tripwire is set BELOW the real danger threshold: subtract the elicitation gap and the between-checkpoint capability jump so safeguards trigger before a checkpoint silently crosses the true line.
new_figures.rationale: The RSP/frontier-safety worked example ('set the tripwire at 70 - 12 - 10 = 48') is the chapter's most quantitative and least intuitive policy idea, and it is the same conservative-floor logic that underlies the whole 'evals are a lower bound' theme. A number-line figure makes the buffer geometry immediate: real threshold, two subtracted margins, tripwire, and a checkpoint sitting between tripwire and threshold that correctly triggers safeguards. Turns a dense paragraph into one glance and cements the asymmetry (cheap false alarm vs catastrophic silent crossing).
new_figures.placement: Section 'Frontier Safety Frameworks & Responsible Scaling', inside/after the note 'Worked example: setting an eval tripwire with a safety buffer'
new_figures.brief: A horizontal capability number line (0 -> 100, 'operational plan completeness / capability score'). Mark the REAL danger threshold at 70 with a bold vertical line labeled 'meaningful real-world uplift (ASL-3 threshold)'. Working leftward from 70, draw two labeled subtraction spans as bracketed segments: 'elicitation gap (~12): attacker fine-tuning + scaffolding extracts more than our in-house eval' and 'between-checkpoint capability jump (~10): capability can rise this much before the next eval'. Land the cumulative subtraction at 48 and draw a second bold vertical line labeled 'TRIPWIRE we set (48)'. Place a checkpoint marker (a distinct dot) at 52, sitting between the tripwire and the real threshold; annotate it 'measured 52: not yet over 70 under naive elicitation, but ABOVE the 48 tripwire -> triggers ASL-3 safeguards NOW'. Shade the band between 48 and 70 as the 'safety buffer'. Add a short caption stating the asymmetry: 'a false alarm is cheap (build safeguards early); a silent crossing above 70 is catastrophic and irreversible.' The only numbers are the given illustrative integers (70, 12, 10, 48, 52) from the chapter's worked example - invent none. Use theme tokens; reserve one accent hue for the tripwire/buffer, a warning tone for the real-threshold line. Keep all labels non-overlapping above/below the line and ensure the full 0-100 axis fits the viewBox. Legible in both light and dark.

summary: Chapter is concept-rich and currently has two figures. oversight-gap-curves is excellent - keep. safety-case-argument-tree needs a refine: body text clips past the right edge of the three cards (visible in dark theme) and card fills are hardcoded white rather than theme-aware; reflow text inside the cards and make fills theme-aware. Three foundational figures are missing: (1) a side-by-side structural comparison of the three scalable-oversight mechanisms (debate / RRM / W2SG) - highest leverage, it carries ~40% of the chapter; (2) the trusted-monitoring control loop that makes the control-vs-alignment 'safety without trust' inversion click; (3) a number-line showing the safety-buffer/tripwire geometry from the RSP worked example. Proposed 3 new figures, all static (no motion is essential here), disciplined to the chapter's biggest conceptual gaps.
